---
title: "Digital Health and the Leapfrog Illusion"
subtitle: "Socio-technical Systems in Global Health"
author: "Sean Sylvia, Ph.D."
date: today
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    css: clean-unc.scss
    filters:
      - reveal-header
    header-logo: images/dhep-logo-2.png
    header-logo-link: https://dhep.unc.edu
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    controls: true
    progress: true
    scroll-view: true
    history: false
    hash-type: number
    multiplex: true
    preview-links: auto
    chalkboard: 
      boardmarker-width: 5
    from: markdown+emoji
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: images/RobotDoctor.png
      data-background-size: cover
      data-background-opacity: "0.2"
---

## The Promise and Peril of Healthcare AI 


### The Promise 

- **By 2030**: AI projected to save US healthcare **$150 billion annually**
- Transformative potential for diagnosis, treatment, prevention
- Leapfrogging opportunity for LMICs

---

## AI as Economic Transformation 

### Economics of Digitization: 

**Reduction in (Marginal) Costs **

| Cost Type | Healthcare Application |
|---------------|----------------------------|
| **Search Costs** | Instant medical literature access, symptom lookup |
| **Replication Costs** | Digital health records, AI models deployed at scale |
| **Transportation Costs** | Telemedicine, remote monitoring, digital consultations |
| **Tracking Costs** | Continuous patient surveillance, automated data collection |
| **Verification Costs** | Digital identity, online reputation systems, trust mechanisms |

---

## Leapfrogging Potential 

**LMIC health systems can choose to follow path of developed countries, or adopt latest technology and processes.**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Well-traveled, but outdated vs. Modern, but risky
    - New path in healthcare delivery enabled by "4th Industrial Revolution" tech:AI, sharing economy, IoT, etc
- Many LMICs have strong incentives to follow new path (WEF 2015)
    - Large pop + "dual disease burden" --> High cost of high-income country approach 
    - 4IR tech low marginal cost
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/mobile.png){width="500"}

*Mobile leapfrogging precedent: LMICs bypassed landline infrastructure*
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The economic lens is crucial - AI fundamentally changes cost structures. The M-Pesa example shows how LMICs can leapfrog traditional infrastructure. Need a figure showing mobile banking adoption curves.
:::


---

## The Peril: AI Often Fails In Healthcare 

| System | Scope | Key Failure | Real-World Impact |
|--------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Epic Sepsis Model** | 56% of US hospitals | Missed 67% of cases, false alerts 88% of the time | Delayed treatment, alert fatigue |
| **IBM Watson Oncology** | Major hospitals globally | Unsafe treatment recommendations | $62M+ wasted, quietly discontinued |
| **SkinVision App** | Consumer market | 235% worse performance with rotation | Missed melanomas, health disparities |
| **COMPAS (Criminal Justice)** | 46 US states | 44.9% vs 23.5% false positive rates | Systematic racial bias in sentencing |

::: {.fragment}
**Pattern:** Technical validation ≠ Real-world success
:::

::: {.notes}
Each row represents millions in investment and thousands of lives affected. The pattern is clear: systems that pass technical validation fail when deployed in complex social systems. Include source citations in appendix.
:::


## Six Challenges to AI Adoption 

![](images/six-challenges.png){width="100%"}


# Why do technically sound AI systems fail in practice? {background-color="#40666e"} 


## Technical excellence is necessary but insufficient


::: {.fragment}
Healthcare AI systems exist within [**complex human and organizational contexts**]{.alert}
:::

::: {.fragment}
We need frameworks that account for [socio-technical dynamics]{.alert}
:::

::: {.fragment}
Need a [**Systematic approach**]{.alert} to design, implement, and evaluate for social welfare optimization
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the core argument. Technical excellence might get you 30% of the way there. The other 70% is understanding human behavior, organizational dynamics, and unintended consequences.
:::

# **The Socio-Technical Imperative in Digital Health** {background-color="#40666e"} 

---

## What is a Socio-Technical System? 

::: {.fragment}
#### [Systems where social and technical elements are fundamentally intertwined]{.alert}
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Healthcare Elements:

🔧 **Technology**  
AI algorithms, devices, platforms

👥 **Practitioners**  
Doctors, nurses, administrators

🏥 **Patients**  
Individuals, families, communities
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### System Context:

📋 **Policies**  
Regulations, protocols, guidelines

💰 **Incentives**  
Financial, professional, social

🔄 **Dynamic Interactions**  
All elements continuously influence each other
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Optimizing technical components alone can lead to system-wide failure**
:::

::: {.notes}
Reference Baxter & Sommerville 2011. Think ecosystem analogy - you can't optimize one species without considering the entire food web. Healthcare is particularly complex because of life-or-death decisions.
:::

---

## Traditional AI Implementation Framework: CRISP-DM  

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="70%"}
::: {.center}
![](images/crisp-dm-slide1.svg){width="1000"}
::: 
:::
::: {.column width="30%"}
::: {.fragment}
<br>

#### Missing Elements:
- Feedback loops 
  - Dynamics 
  - Causality
- Multiple stakeholders 
  - Behavioral adaptation 

:::
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
CRISP-DM from Chapman et al. 2000. Excellent for traditional business intelligence but assumes you can build and deploy without changing underlying data generating process. Almost never true in healthcare/social systems.
:::


## CRISP-DM Extended: Socio-Technical Systems 

::: {.center}
![](images/crisp-dm-slide2.svg){width="1000"}
:::

::: {.fragment}
[**Key Addition:** Feedback Loops. Models change the world they operate in]{.alert}
:::

::: {.notes}
This shows how we need to extend CRISP-DM to account for socio-technical realities. The red feedback loop is critical - deployment fundamentally changes the data generating process.
:::

---

## Facilitates Cross-Disciplinary Collaboration 

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
#### Economics & Behavioral Science 
- Incentives 
- Market mechanisms 
- Behavioral economics
- Game theory applications
- Decision-making under uncertainty

:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
#### Expertise

- Implementation science
- Clinical
- Biomedical
- Data Engineering and Architecture
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
#### Causal Inference
- Moving beyond correlation
- Identification strategies
- Confounding control
- Treatment effect estimation

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Reference Pearl 2009 for causal inference. This is about building a new type of researcher/practitioner. Not enough to be great at ML or great at economics - need systems thinking across disciplines.
:::

---

## Framework for LLM deployment in Healthcare Settings

![](images/npj-AI-Audit-Figure.png)


# Case Study: The Sepsis Prediction Paradox {background-color="#13294B"} 


## Setting the Scene 

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Clinical Challenge
- **Sepsis**: Leading cause of hospital deaths globally
- **1.7 million cases** annually in US alone
- **$62 billion** in healthcare costs
- **Time-critical**: Each hour delay = 7.6% mortality increase
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### AI Potential
- Early detection saves lives
- Reduce alert fatigue  
- Standardize care quality
- Massive cost savings through prevention
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
[Epic's model deployed across hundreds of hospitals despite **67% miss rate**]{.alert}
:::

::: {.notes}
Sepsis is perfect case study - high stakes, time pressure, complex symptoms that mimic other conditions. AI seems like obvious solution, but implementation has been deeply problematic. The statistics come from multiple validation studies.
:::

---

## Sepsis-specific Socio-Technical Framework 

::: {.center}
![](images/crisp-dm-slide3.svg){width="850"}
:::

::: {.fragment}
[**Selection Bias** <br>
AI recommendations → Treatment decisions → Censored outcomes → Biased retraining]{.alert}
:::

::: {.notes}
This concrete example shows how sepsis prediction systems create the very bias they're trying to avoid. Group B (majority, high-risk) develops severe prediction bias because their outcomes get censored by treatment.
:::

---

## The Agents & Dynamics

#### Three Physician Types:

- Never-takers (20%): Ignore AI recommendations
- Always-takers (30%): Follow AI blindly
- Bayesians (50%): Integrate AI with clinical judgment

#### Dynamics:

- Trust in AI evolves over time
- Different treatment patterns by patient demographics
- Alert fatigue develops with false positives
- Hospital culture affects adoption

::: {.fragment}
**Key Insight:** Feedback loops between AI, physicians, and outcomes. System behavior emerges from interactions
:::


::: {.notes}
The three physician types aren't arbitrary - they represent real behavioral patterns from clinical studies. The percentages are estimates from literature and our modeling work. The key insight is that system behavior emerges from interactions.
:::

---

## The Feedback Loop Catastrophe 

**Selective Labels Bias Process**

1. AI recommends treatment
2. Physicians follow recommendations selectively 
3. Treated patients → censored outcomes
4. Biased training data for next iteration
5. Performance degrades for untreated groups


::: {.fragment}

#### Simulation Results: 10 iterations (Particular set of assumptions)

| Group | Population % | Treatment Rate | Bias  |
|-------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Minority | 30% | 85% | +0.1 |
| Majority | 70% | 95% | **-0.9** |

**Key Finding:** Majority group experiences severe performance degradation
:::

::: {.fragment}
[**Deployment changes the data-generating process**]{.alert}
:::

::: {.notes}
This is our core theoretical contribution. The math shows that once treatment depends on AI recommendations AND group membership, you get biased training data. Group B represents the majority population that gets treated more often, leading to worse model performance for them.
:::

---

## Key Insights from Sepsis Case 

::: {.incremental}
1. **Deployment fundamentally changes the data-generating process**
   - Traditional ML assumes training ≈ deployment distribution
   - Leads to ["Endogenous Data Drift"]{.alert}

2. **Sub-populations experience different model performance**
   - Feedback loops amplify disparities over time

3. **Traditional evaluation metrics miss these dynamics**
   - Need new metrics that account for distribution shift
   - System-level outcomes vs. model-level performance

4. **Next Steps: Engineering solutions**
   - Develop bias decomposition methods
   - Design intervention protocols
   - Create robust evaluation frameworks
:::

::: {.notes}
These insights drive our research agenda. We're not just documenting problems - we're developing systematic solutions that account for socio-technical realities.
:::


# System Design {background-color="#13294B"}

## Two DHEP Lab Projects 

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

#### Project A: Optimizing Clinical Case Presentation 

**Challenge:** Information overload in clinical decision-making

**Approach:** Behavioral economics + AI

**Focus:** AI that complements clinical expertise by optimizing information presentation

:::
::: {.column}

#### Project B: Information Markets for Epidemiological Surveillance

**Challenge:** Traditional surveillance systems are slow and expensive

**Approach:** Community-based participatory surveillance using prediction markets + AI 

**Focus:** Cost-effective signal of disease and socioeconomic trends at the community level

:::

:::

---

## Key Takeaways 

#### 1. Healthcare AI is Inherently Socio-Technical 

- Technical metrics alone insufficient
- Must consider [feedback loops]{.alert}
- [Context determines success]{.alert} in addition to algorithm choice

::: {.fragment}
#### 2. Frameworks Must Evolve

- From [static/one-way processes]{.alert} to [dynamic systems]{.alert}
- From [single objectives]{.alert} to [multi-stakeholder optimization]{.alert}
- From [build/deploy]{.alert} to [maintain/evolve]{.alert}

:::

---

## Key Takeaways - Continued 

#### 3. Equity Implications are Paramount
- Feedback loops can [amplify disparities]{.alert}
- Need [proactive design]{.alert} for fairness
- [Community engagement]{.alert} as methodology, not just ethics

#### 4. New Evaluation Metrics Needed
- Beyond accuracy: [fairness, robustness, adaptability]{.alert}
- [System-level outcomes]{.alert}, not just model performance
- [Long-term impact assessment]{.alert}


::: {.notes}
These aren't just nice-to-haves - they're fundamental requirements for AI systems that actually improve health outcomes rather than optimizing narrow technical metrics while causing broader harm.
:::

---

## Implications - Research 

### Methodological Advances Needed

- [Causal models]{.alert} of human-AI interaction
- Methods for [robust optimization]{.alert} under distribution shift  
- [Participatory design methods]{.alert} at scale
- [Evaluation frameworks]{.alert} for dynamic systems
- [Cross-disciplinary training]{.alert} programs

---

## Many Open Research Questions 

::: {.incremental}
1. How do we evaluate AI systems that change their environment?

2. What are the fundamental limits of prediction in adaptive systems?

3. How do we design AI systems that reduce rather than amplify disparities?

4. How should we regulate systems that evolve continuously after deployment?

5. Who bears responsibility when AI systems cause unintended harm through feedback loops?

6. How do we align individual incentives with collective welfare in AI systems?
:::

::: {.notes}
These questions will define the field for the next decade. Each requires interdisciplinary collaboration. The meta-question about training is particularly important - we need institutional changes in how we educate researchers.
:::

---

## Implications - Practice

### Implementation Principles

- Move beyond "accuracy" as primary metric
- Design for [system health]{.alert}, not just model performance
- Engage communities as [partners]{.alert}, not data sources
- Plan for [behavioral adaptation]{.alert} from day one
- Informative AI audit and evaluation metrics

---

## Implications - Policy
### Regulatory Innovation

- Oversight for [dynamic, adaptive systems]{.alert}
- [Continuous monitoring]{.alert} requirements
- [Algorithmic impact assessments]{.alert}
- [Community consent]{.alert} frameworks

::: {.notes}
This requires changes at every level - from individual research practices to institutional funding priorities to regulatory frameworks. Each stakeholder has specific roles to play.
:::


---

## Reflection 

### 1. How might your AI system change behavior?

::: {.fragment}
- What **incentives** does it create?
- Who **adapts** and how?
- What **unintended consequences** are possible?
:::

---

## Reflection 

### 2. What feedback loops exist in your domain?

::: {.fragment}
- How does **deployment change** data distribution?
- Who **benefits** and who is **harmed**?
- What **metrics** would detect these changes?
:::

---

## Reflection 

### 3. How do we regulate dynamic AI systems?

::: {.fragment}
- Traditional oversight assumes **static systems**
- What **new approaches** do we need?
- How do we balance **innovation** and **protection**?
:::

---

## {background-color="#13294B"}


::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
::: {.center} 
![](images/dhep-logo-2.png){width="1000"}
:::
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
<br>
<br>
<br>
**Sean Sylvia, Ph.D.** <br>
<br>
[sean.sylvia@unc.edu](mailto:sean.sylvia@unc.edu) <br>
<br>
[dhep.org](https://dhep.org) <br>
<br>
:::
:::
:::
